title.zh: 氣候變遷的時間陷阱：美國媒體如何塑造我們的「行動倒數計時」？
title.en: The Temporal Paradox of Climate Change: How U.S. Media Shapes Our Countdown to Action

summary.zh:
這項發表於《美國社會學評論》的研究，首度大規模分析2000至2021年間美國媒體如何框架氣候變遷的時間性。透過創新的文本計算方法，團隊發現三個關鍵現象：首先，氣候影響的預測時間點持續前移，但詭異地穩定錨定在2060年這個『魔幻數字』。

其次，儘管災難倒數加速，『行動時間窗』卻像被凍結般始終維持在16年後——這種『永遠的16年』現象，宛如社會集體拖延症的時間膠囊。最矛盾的是第三點：在加州野火等極端事件期間，媒體會爆發『立即行動』的緊急話語，但這些聲浪退去後，長期行動框架又回歸靜止狀態。

研究揭露了時間敘事中的斷裂：我們愈來愈頻繁地『感覺』火燒眉毛，卻始終『規劃』在安全距離外行動。這種時間認知的分裂，或許正是氣候行動遲滯的隱形癥結。
summary.en:
This groundbreaking study in the American Sociological Review unveils how U.S. media has framed the temporality of climate change from 2000 to 2021. Using innovative computational text analysis, researchers discovered a troubling temporal disconnect: while predicted impacts creep steadily closer (consistently centering on the year 2060), the perceived window for taking action remains stubbornly fixed at about 16 years into the future—a phenomenon the authors term 'the 16-year horizon trap.'

During acute climate disasters like the 2020 California wildfires, media narratives erupt with urgent calls for immediate action. Yet these spikes of urgency fail to shift the underlying long-term temporal framework. The study identifies this paradox: society increasingly perceives climate threats as imminent while continuing to defer substantive action to a perpetually receding future.

By exposing this temporal disjuncture—where visceral urgency and procedural delay coexist—the research provides a novel lens for understanding climate inaction. It challenges prevailing assumptions about how temporal framing influences collective response to slow-moving crises, offering critical insights for communicators and policymakers alike.

keyArticle.title: Time and Climate Change: U.S. Media Representations of Climate Actions, Horizons, and Events (2000 to 2021)
